channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Pirate Borg Starter Set Review - Failing Never Felt So Fun
item.content:encoded:
Shipwrecks, haunted sea shanties, cursed treasure… what the hell else do you need? There’s a brand new starter set just out for Pirate Borg, the pirate-themed hack of the popular dark punk fantasy RPG MÖRK BORG, and publisher Free League sent this salty sea dawg a copy to check out. Wanna know if it floats or sinks? Follow along for my review of the Pirate Borg Starter Set.

What Is Pirate Borg?

You know how MÖRK BORG is this chaotic, wild, gritty, down-low, heavy-metal-inspired, easy to jump into fantasy Tabletop RPG? Imagine that… but with pirates. That’s Pirate Borg! Developed by Limithron, and unaffiliated with the makers of the original game (they’re using the incredible MÖRK BORG Third Party License, Pirate Borg charts new waters while embracing the original game’s core mechanics.

In the game, as with most TTRPG’s, one player takes on the role of the GM, steering the story and taking on the various NPC’s, enemies, shipwrecking storms, and more, while the other players play as pirates heading out into the Dark Caribbean (the game’s standard setting), seeking fame and fortune, grog and treasure.

How Does Character Creation Work In Pirate Borg?

Character creation in Pirate Borg couldn’t be easier, with much of the character build surrounding dice rolls. To start, and I find this an interesting and fun quirk of the system, the very first thing you’ll roll for your characters is a bit of starting gear. You’ll find out what you’ve got to carry your stuff around (from a bucket to a dinghy!), a piece of common gear, and a piece of fancy gear.

From there you’ll roll your ability scores (that’s right, no point-buy here!) and go straight down the line for stats! Those stats (and their in-book descriptions) are: Strength (crush, lift, strike, grapple), Agility (defend, balance, swim, flee), Presence (smarts, ranged weapons, perceive, charm), Toughness (drink grog, hold breath, survive falling), and Spirit (willpower, using relics, casting rituals).

This is gloriously old-school feeling, as (if you’re playing purely by the rules), you’ll just start with Strength, roll 3d6, and get what ya get - moving on down the stats! From there, you roll for your class - meaning you could whip up an absolute weakling, and then for your class roll end up with a Brute (the fighter-analog).

Character Classes Shine Like Doubloons

There are six core classes to the game you roll for, which are as follows: Brute, who specializes in beating down your enemies and comes with a special (randomly rolled) starting weapon. Rapscallion, a sneaky lil dude. Buccaneer, who loves firearms and explosives. Swashbuckler, who’s an all around good fighter and focuses in on one particular fighting style. Zealot, an absolute religious freak. And Sorcerer, a weak but potent magic wielder.

From there, two bonus character classes emerge if your group decides to use them (because they can be game/immersion breaking). First, there’s the Haunted Soul - “a possessed, infected, cursed, troubled, or undead soul.” Random rolls after rolling up this class could mean you end up being a ghost, skeleton, etc.

Finally, there’s the most chaotic of all classes, the “Tall Tale,” which could see you rolling up a Merfolk, an Aquatic Mutant, or my favorite, a Sentient Animal. Yes, with a few easy rolls you could end up being a talking chicken, diseased (talking) rat, parrot, and more.

The Chaos Is The Point

If these classes, plus the inability to actually choose how you spend your points, feels chaotic - good! It’s supposed to! This is where the game shines - it wants things to be a little bit hitchy feeling, it wants you to play suboptimal characters - or get lucky and roll the perfect character - because either way it invites clever roleplaying.

Think about it, if I roll a sorcerer with an extremely low Spirit score… what kind of character will that be? Conversely, if I roll an extremely agile Rapscallion with high presence, the very nature of my good rolls will lead me to treat this character like she’s something special. That’s the power of the Mörk and Pirate Borg systems: harnessing randomization and channelling that into good game design.

Naval Combat Feels Fluid (Get It?)

Another important element to Pirate Borg, as you’d expect from any good game about buccaneers, is ship to ship combat. There are rules for crewing a ship that are clever and intuitive, and rules for how your PC’s can help in ship to ship combat (before getting close enough to jump aboard the enemy’s ship, of course).

Movement makes far more sense to me than it ever did in other games where vessel movement was important (cough cough - Spelljammer), and there are even cut outs included in the Starter Set to help you visualize naval combat. Which brings me to…

What Else Is Included In The Pirate Borg Starter Set?

All the other stuff that comes in this box! Also included in the Pirate Borg starter set is everything you need to quickly get into the action and get on your way sailing the seven seas.

There are over 50 different tokens representing PC’s, enemies, ships, and more, along with ten large poster-sized maps, 25 character sheets, a full set of dice, dry erase markers (which you can use on laminated PC creation sheets, and - best of all - a complete adventure to ease players into the game.

The Trapped In The Tropics Adventure Brings It All Home

The adventure included in the Pirate Borg starter set, Trapped in the Tropics, is one of the most intriguing, twist-y beginner adventures I’ve ever laid my landlubbing eyes on!

In it, your crew has been hired to find a shipwreck lost somewhere on a jungle island, but there are all kinds of threats around. Other pirates have their own goals for the area, the locals of some of the islands in the area hate pirates, the ever-annoying British Redcoats are prowling around… oh, and there are zombies! Of course!

Each movement of the adventure is meant to slowly immerse players into the world of Pirate Borg without bogging them down with different concepts, and you start land-locked for the first section, searching around a jungle island for clues.

It’s not until you’ve got your feet well under you that you get access to a boat - which opens the game up and introduces sailing. And after that, well… I don’t want to spoil anything, but let’s just say things get a little (more) supernatural!

Pirate Borg Starter Set - Final Thoughts

To say the Pirate Borg starter set is a massive achievement would be a true understatement. This is one of the most comprehensive, enjoyable RPG gaming experiences I’ve had in a long time.

The copy of Pirate Borg used in the creation of this review was provided by Free League Publishing. Additional review items not directly featured in this review (extra dice, tokens, adventures, etc.) were provided by Limithron.

Giaco Furino joined the TechRaptor team as a Staff Writer in 2019 after searching for a dedicated place to write and talk about Tabletop Games. In 2020, he… More about Giaco
